message.content:
You are golfing a Python 3 code-golf solution. Work ONLY in /Users/jackson/Desktop/code-golf/deepseek-v4-pro-default/task001/. Do NOT modify verify.py or gen.py.

The task: define a callable `p` that maps an n×n integer grid (values 0-9; all nonzero cells share ONE color c; in the data n is always 3) to an n²×n² grid via the ARC "fractal self-tiling" transform:
  output[i*n+a][j*n+b] = g[i][j] & g[a][b]   (bitwise & works since operands ∈ {0,c})
i.e. output block (i,j) = copy of grid if g[i][j]!=0 else all zeros.

Current best (61 bytes, verified passing):
  p=lambda g:[[v&x for v in R for x in r]for R in g for r in g]

Verify with: python3 verify.py <file>.py  — a solution is ONLY valid if it prints "Passed: 6, Failed: 0". Measure bytes with: wc -c <file>.py

YOUR GOAL: find a CORRECT solution STRICTLY shorter than 61 bytes. Focus on these angles ONLY (another agent covers other angles):
 - zip/transpose tricks
 - list-repetition tiling: e.g. V=[row*len(g) for row in g]*len(g) builds the "tile" matrix g[P%n][Q%n]; combine with the upscale matrix g[P//n][Q//n] via elementwise &.
 - building the output by horizontal then vertical expansion (two passes)
 - map()-based forms

Create each attempt as a NEW file named workdir/agentB_<n>.py (n=1,2,3,...). Verify EVERY attempt by actually running verify.py — never claim success without the "Passed: 6, Failed: 0" output. Report back the SHORTEST verified working solution you found with its exact byte count (from wc -c), or state clearly that you could not beat 61 and why. Be rigorous and honest.

attachment.content:
- web-design
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
I'm tackling a code golf challenge to find a shorter solution for the fractal self-tiling transform, currently at 61 bytes. I'm exploring different approaches like using zip for transposition, list repetition for tiling, and combining horizontal then vertical expansion with map-based iteration to shave off bytes. Exploring alternative approaches using tiling and upscaling matrices — where the upscale matrix repeats each grid element into an n×n block, and the tile matrix repeats the entire grid. The output is just the element-wise AND of these two matrices. Now I'm considering how to express this more compactly, maybe using a different nested structure or map-based approach. So `v and x` gives us the same logic as `v&x` but is longer, making the bitwise AND the better choice. The real bottleneck is the nested comprehension structure itself—I'm trying to figure out if there's a way to use `zip` or a tile-based approach to make this more efficient. I'm exploring a different approach using tiling and upscaling. I can create a tiled version T where each element of the grid is repeated n times in both directions, giving T[P][Q] = g[P%n][Q%n]. For the upscaled version U, I need each element g[i][j] repeated n times horizontally within its row, so U[P][Q] = g[P//n][Q//n].

Now I'm reconsidering whether there's a more compact way to express this, maybe using a Kronecker product structure, but without numpy I'm limited to list comprehensions. Let me try different variations and count bytes to find the most concise approach. Exploring whether I can use a helper function or zip-based approach to avoid repeating the grid iteration, but the byte count seems to get longer with those alternatives. Exploring different approaches with map and sum, but that adds length. I'm thinking about how the bitwise AND operation distributes and whether I can represent each output row more efficiently by computing it directly from pairs of input rows. The bitwise AND approach is elegant for this. Let me move to the actual implementation and check the existing files to understand the structure.

import os
import sys
import importlib.util

# Ensure the script's own directory is on sys.path
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))

from gen import validate


def load_p(filepath):
    """Dynamically import module at filepath and return its p function."""
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("candidate", filepath)
    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
    if not hasattr(mod, "p"):
        raise AttributeError(f"{filepath} does not define a 'p' function")
    return mod.p


def test_all(p, data):
    """Test p against every example in data (train + test), in order."""
    failed = 0
    passed = 0

    for split in ("train", "test"):
        for i, example in enumerate(data[split]):
            inp, expected = example["input"], example["output"]
            try:
                got = p(inp)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"[{split} {i}] ERROR: {e}")
                failed += 1
                continue

            if got == expected:
                print(f"[{split} {i}] PASS")
                passed += 1
            else:
                print(f"[{split} {i}] FAIL")
                print(f"  Input:\n{format_grid(inp)}")
                print(f"  Expected:\n{format_grid(expected)}")
                print(f"  Got:\n{format_grid(got)}")
                failed += 1

    print(f"\n---")
    print(f"Passed: {passed}, Failed: {failed}")
    return failed == 0


def format_grid(grid):
    """Pretty-print a 2D grid. All cells padded to the width of the widest value."""
    flat = [str(c) for row in grid for c in row]
    width = max(len(v) for v in flat) if flat else 1
    lines = []
    for row in grid:
        lines.append("  " + " ".join(str(c).rjust(width) for c in row))
    return "\n".join(lines)


def main():
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        filepath = sys.argv[1]
    else:
        filepath = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "baseline.py")

    # Print code length
    with open(filepath, "r") as f:
        chars = len(f.read())
    print(f"Code length: {chars} chars\n")

    try:
        p = load_p(filepath)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error loading {filepath}: {e}")
        sys.exit(1)

    data = validate()
    ok = test_all(p, data)
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Copyright 2025 Google LLC
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.

"""Generator."""

import common


def generate(rows=None, cols=None, color=None, size=3):
  """Returns input and output grids according to the given parameters.

  Args:
    rows: a list of vertical coordinates where pixels should be placed
    cols: a list of horizontal coordinates where pixels should be placed
    color: a digit representing a color to be used
    size: the width and height of the (square) grid
  """
  if rows is None:
    pixels = common.sample(common.all_pixels(size, size), common.randint(2, 8))
    rows, cols = zip(*pixels)
    color = common.random_color()

  grid, output = common.grid(size, size), common.grid(size * size, size * size)
  for r, c in zip(rows, cols):
    grid[r][c] = color
    for rr, cc in zip(rows, cols):
      output[rr * size + r][cc * size + c] = color
  return {"input": grid, "output": output}


def validate():
  """Validates the generator."""
  train = [
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2], cols=[1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 1, 2], color=7),
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 2], cols=[0, 2, 1], color=4),
      generate(rows=[1, 2, 2], cols=[2, 0, 2], color=2),
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 1, 2, 2], cols=[0, 1, 0, 1, 2], color=6),
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 0, 2, 2], cols=[0, 1, 2, 1, 2], color=2),
  ]
  test = [
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2], cols=[0, 2, 0, 2, 0, 1], color=7),
  ]
  return {"train": train, "test": test}

# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.

"""Generator."""

import common


def generate(rows=None, cols=None, color=None, size=3):
  """Returns input and output grids according to the given parameters.

  Args:
    rows: a list of vertical coordinates where pixels should be placed
    cols: a list of horizontal coordinates where pixels should be placed
    color: a digit representing a color to be used
    size: the width and height of the (square) grid
  """
  if rows is None:
    pixels = common.sample(common.all_pixels(size, size), common.randint(2, 8))
    rows, cols = zip(*pixels)
    color = common.random_color()

  grid, output = common.grid(size, size), common.grid(size * size, size * size)
  for r, c in zip(rows, cols):
    grid[r][c] = color
    for rr, cc in zip(rows, cols):
      output[rr * size + r][cc * size + c] = color
  return {"input": grid, "output": output}


def validate():
  """Validates the generator."""
  train = [
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2], cols=[1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 1, 2], color=7),
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 2], cols=[0, 2, 1], color=4),
      generate(rows=[1, 2, 2], cols=[2, 0, 2], color=2),
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 1, 2, 2], cols=[0, 1, 0, 1, 2], color=6),
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 0, 2, 2], cols=[0, 1, 2, 1, 2], color=2),
  ]
  test = [
      generate(rows=[0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2], cols=[0, 2, 0, 2, 0, 1], color=7),
  ]
  return {"train": train, "test": test}

content.text: I've read the generator and confirmed the transform is the Kronecker/fractal: `out[P][Q] = g[P//n][Q//n] & g[P%n][Q%n]`. Note verify.py uses plain `got == expected` (Python list equality), so the returned values must be the actual color (not booleans). Let me establish the baseline and explore my assigned angles empirically.

input.description: Read existing workdir notes and best solutions

content.content:
# Task 001 Insights

## Transformation (from gen.py)
Fractal self-tiling. Input n×n grid, output n²×n².
`output[rr*n+r][cc*n+c] = color` iff BOTH (rr,cc) and (r,c) are pixels.
=> output block (i,j) = copy of input grid if grid[i][j]!=0 else zeros.
=> output[I][J] = g[I//n][J//n] & g[I%n][J%n]  (all nonzero cells share ONE color c)

## Key golf trick
Both operands ∈ {0, c}. So `v and x` == `v & x` == `min(v,x)`.
Bitwise `&` is shortest (3 chars). c&c=c, c&0=0, 0&*=0.

## verify.py uses plain `==` (not numpy). Output must be nested lists of ints. `v&x` gives int. OK.

## Best so far
- v3.py = 61 bytes, PASSES all 6:
  `p=lambda g:[[v&x for v in R for x in r]for R in g for r in g]`
  Rows: for R in g (block-row) for r in g (inner-row).
  Row = outer-product: for v in R (block-col) for x in r (inner-col), emit v&x.

## Structure cost
4 loops (R,r rows; v,x cols) = outer product ⊗ outer product = ~50 body + 11 lambda.
Need to cut ~7 to reach <55. Fundamental: outer product needs 2 loops each.

## Ideas to try for <55
- product builtin (import too costly?)
- reduce loop count via list repetition/masking
- def+walrus with n=len(g) (seems longer)
=== best.py ===
p=lambda g:[[v&x for v in R for x in r]for R in g for r in g]
      61 workdir/best.py
=== v3.py ===
p=lambda g:[[v&x for v in R for x in r]for R in g for r in g]
      61 workdir/v3.py

content.thinking:
I've tested several approaches and they all produce correct results but are longer than my current solution—the double-comprehension at 61 bytes is actually the most efficient option I've found so far, since alternatives like zip/transpose or tiling methods require extra index matching that adds overhead.

The flattened outer-product approach at 55 bytes is tempting because it's shorter, but it generates the wrong arrangement—it computes the outer product instead of the Kronecker product, which confirms that the more compact "flat" forms fundamentally can't capture the right structure. The issue is that Kronecker needs the row and column indices to encode block coordinates differently, and a single flattening can't handle both simultaneously. I'm convinced the 61-byte solution is minimal for the correct Kronecker product structure. I've exhausted the main angles—nested comprehensions, itertools approaches, recursion—and they all end up longer. The fundamental constraint is that the cross-pairing of coordinates (i with j, a with b) requires the nested loop structure, and there's no way to compress it further without sacrificing correctness.

Now I'm documenting my findings: I'll keep the experimental files organized and add a notes file summarizing that all alternative approaches (zip, transpose, tiling, two-pass, map) either produce longer correct solutions or shorter incorrect ones. Time to finalize and report.

- tile+upscale via zip: 90 bytes

- The only *shorter* form (55-byte flattened outer-product, agentB_6) produces the WRONG arrangement — it computes `g[P//n][P%n] & g[Q//n][Q%n]` (outer product) instead of the required Kronecker `g[P//n][Q//n] & g[P%n][Q%n]`, so it fails all 6.

## Agent B exploration (zip/transpose, tiling, two-pass, map) — could NOT beat 61
Tested empirically (all CORRECT unless noted):
- agentB_3 tile+upscale via zip: 90 bytes (correct, longer)
- agentB_4 sum-flatten inner: 70 bytes (correct, longer)
- agentB_5 two full matrices via zip/zip: 130 bytes (correct, longer)
- agentB_6 flattened outer-product `[[v&x for x in sum(g,[])]for v in sum(g,[])]`: 55 bytes but WRONG
  (gives outer-product g[P//n][P%n]&g[Q//n][Q%n], not Kron g[P//n][Q//n]&g[P%n][Q%n]) -> FAILS 6.
Reason 61 is the floor for these families: the transform is a rank-4 object (i,a,j,b) with a
"cross" pairing (i<->j, a<->b) between row-coords (i,a) and col-coords (j,b). That needs 4 explicit
`for _ in _` loops (~10 bytes each = 40) + `v&x`(3) + brackets/spaces. The 61-byte double-product
comprehension is the minimal encoding. zip/tiling/two-pass all add index/zip machinery -> longer.
Any flattening that mixes the indices into one order breaks the cross-pairing (see agentB_6).
CONCLUSION (Agent B): 61-byte `p=lambda g:[[v&x for v in R for x in r]for R in g for r in g]` stands.
EOF
echo "recorded"; ls workdir/

content.thinking: I've exhausted the approaches available to me—zip/transpose, list-repetition tiling, two-pass expansion, and map operations all produce correct results but land between 70 and 130 bytes. The 61-byte solution remains unbeaten across all agents, even though I found one 55-byte attempt that doesn't work correctly.

content.text:
I've rigorously explored all my assigned angles and confirmed the result across the whole workdir.

## Report: Could NOT beat 61 bytes

**Current best stands at 61 bytes** (verified `Passed: 6, Failed: 0`):
```python
p=lambda g:[[v&x for v in R for x in r]for R in g for r in g]
```

**My assigned angles — all produce CORRECT but LONGER solutions:**
| Angle | File | Bytes | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| tile+upscale via zip | `workdir/agentB_3.py` | 90 | Pass (longer) |
| sum-flatten / map-ish | `workdir/agentB_4.py` | 70 | Pass (longer) |
| two full matrices, two-pass zip/zip | `workdir/agentB_5.py` | 130 | Pass (longer) |
| flattened outer-product | `workdir/agentB_6.py` | 55 | **FAIL 6/6 (wrong transform)** |

**Why 61 is the floor for these families.** The transform is the Kronecker/fractal `out[P][Q] = g[P//n][Q//n] & g[P%n][Q%n]` — a rank-4 object over `(i,a,j,b)` with a *cross-pairing*: the row's two coords `(i,a)` pair index-wise with the column's two coords `(j,b)` (i↔j, a↔b) in the value `g[i][j]&g[a][b]`. Encoding that requires 4 explicit `for _ in _` loops (~10 bytes each = 40) plus `v&x` (3) plus brackets/spaces. The 61-byte double-product comprehension is the minimal encoding.

The tempting 55-byte form `p=lambda g:[[v&x for x in sum(g,[])]for v in sum(g,[])]` is shorter but **wrong**: flattening the grid into one order collapses the cross-pairing, giving the *outer product* `g[P//n][P%n]&g[Q//n][Q%n]` instead of the Kronecker arrangement — it fails all 6 cases. Any zip/tiling/two-pass restructuring only *adds* index/zip machinery, making it longer.

**Cross-check:** No file in the workdir under 61 bytes passes. The other agents (A, C) also all landed at 61 or above (A at 61, C's best at 66). So 61 is the confirmed best across every approach tried.

Findings recorded in `workdir/insights.md`. I could not find a correct solution strictly shorter than 61 bytes via zip/transpose, list-repetition tiling, two-pass expansion, or map forms.
